title: How Does a Computer Think? The PB&J Sandwich Lesson
description: Students discover that computers follow exact step-by-step instructions, and that sequence determines the result.

roleModelOfDay.name: Ada Lovelace
roleModelOfDay.intro: The world’s first computer programmer — she wrote algorithms for a machine that didn’t exist yet.

vocabulary.def: What comes out (the sandwich, the route, the result)

learningObjectives: Explain in their own words that a computer follows instructions exactly, one step at a time.
Describe why sequence matters by showing how changing the order changes the outcome.
Use key vocabulary correctly: algorithm, input, output, and sequence.
Debug unclear instructions by testing and improving a written algorithm.
materials: Bread, peanut butter, and jam (or printed ingredient cards for allergy-safe version)
fullDescription: Age group: Groep 5-8 (ages 8-12). Duration: 45-60 minutes. Devices required: computers for exercise phase (webapp). SLO alignment: Domain A (21A), Domain B (22B). Students experience computational thinking through a literal robot activity and a PB&J challenge. The class learns that computers do not guess, every step must be explicit, and order drives output. Role model of the day: Ada Lovelace, who wrote the first algorithm designed for a machine long before modern computers.

teacherInstructions.setup: Write vocabulary on the board and keep it visible all lesson: Algorithm, Input, Output, Sequence. Place PB&J materials (or picture cards) at the front table. Prepare paper for pair activities.
steps: Phase 1 (0-3 min): Spark question. Ask: 'What could a robot do better than a human, and what could it never do?' Students share in pairs, then collect 3-4 class answers without correcting.
Phase 2 (3-10 min): Unplugged warm-up (Robot Teacher). Allow only four commands: STEP FORWARD, TURN LEFT, TURN RIGHT, STOP. Follow instructions literally and discuss why precision was hard.
Phase 3 (10-20 min): Concept intro. Define algorithm and explain input, output, sequence. Use real examples (recipe, morning routine, LEGO) and connect to GPS/recommendation mistakes.

Phase 4 (20-42 min): PB&J robot challenge. Round 1 class gives instructions while teacher executes literally. Round 2 pairs write a numbered precise algorithm. Round 3 test one pair algorithm and debug in real time.
Phase 5 (42-47 min): Discussion. Use at least one reflection question about responsibility for errors and revisit opening robot-vs-human ideas.
Exercises (47-57 min): Ask students to get their computers out and open the webapp. They complete the algorithm exercise individually on the webapp.
Share and reflect (52-57 min): Students share one step, class spots vagueness, and each student completes: 'An algorithm needs to be ____ because computers cannot ____.'
discussionPrompts: A robot followed your instructions exactly and got the wrong result. Whose fault is that: robot or programmer? Why?
When a GPS gives a technically valid but strange route, is that a robot problem or an algorithm design problem?
Return to the opening question: what can robots do better than humans, and what can they never do? Did your view change?
Is there always one correct sequence, or can some algorithms allow multiple valid orders?

teacherGuide: Core message: computers execute instructions exactly, without common sense. Keep vocabulary visible all lesson. Use humor and literal execution during PB&J to surface hidden assumptions, then move into debugging. Prioritize depth over speed in discussion: one strong reflection is better than rushed coverage.

studentExercises.title: Write the Perfect Algorithm
studentExercises.description: Individually write a numbered algorithm (at least 6 steps, one action per step) for one task: getting ready for school, making chocolate milk, or feeding a pet. Then swap and debug with a partner.
instructions: Choose one task: getting ready for school, making chocolate milk, or feeding a pet.
Write at least 6 numbered steps.
Each line must contain one action only.
Make every step precise enough for a robot that cannot guess.
Swap with a partner and mark unclear steps with a question mark.
Improve unclear steps and submit your final algorithm.
successCriteria: Algorithm has 6 or more numbered steps.
Each step is one clear action.
A partner can follow the algorithm without guessing.

studentExercises.title: Sequence Sorting: Make Tea
studentExercises.description: Order six shuffled instruction cards into a logical sequence, compare with a partner, and discuss where sequence is fixed versus flexible.
instructions: Read the six tea-making instruction cards on the webapp.
Drag them into the order you think is correct.
Compare your order with a partner.
If you disagree, explain why your sequence works.
Discuss which steps are fixed and which can be flexible.
successCriteria: Students can justify their sequence choices.
Students identify at least one flexible step.
Students connect sequence changes to output changes.

studentExercises.title: Extension: Robot Chef Algorithm
studentExercises.description: For fast finishers: write a canteen robot algorithm for one lunch item, then extend it with allergy safety and missing-ingredient handling.
instructions: Choose one lunch item from the school canteen menu.
Write a precise step-by-step algorithm a robot chef could follow.
Add safety instructions for students with nut allergies.
Add a rule for what the robot should do if an ingredient runs out.
Challenge: make your algorithm work for any sandwich type.
successCriteria: Safety and missing ingredient edge cases are included.
Student can explain how to generalize the algorithm.

studentChallenges.title: Allergy-Safe Robot Chef
studentChallenges.description: Design a robot-chef algorithm that safely handles a nut allergy without using common sense.
instructions: Choose one canteen food item.
Write a step-by-step robot algorithm for preparing it.
Add explicit allergy checks before handling ingredients.
Include safe fallback steps if risky ingredients appear.
Test your algorithm by having another group try to follow it literally.
successCriteria: Another group can follow it without guessing.
Team can explain why each safety step is needed.

studentChallenges.title: What If an Ingredient Runs Out?
studentChallenges.description: Improve your algorithm with a decision path for missing ingredients during preparation.
instructions: Take an algorithm you already wrote.
Pick one ingredient that could run out halfway.
Add precise steps for what the robot does next.
Include at least one IF style instruction in plain language.
Run a peer test to see whether the updated logic works.
successCriteria: Algorithm has a clear missing-ingredient response.
Decision path is precise and testable.
Peer tester can execute both normal and missing-ingredient scenarios.

studentChallenges.title: One Algorithm for Any Sandwich
studentChallenges.description: Generalize from one sandwich to a reusable algorithm that works with different inputs.
instructions: Rewrite your sandwich algorithm so it does not mention only peanut butter and jam.
Use placeholders like SPREAD A and SPREAD B.
Define input choices at the top (bread type, spread 1, spread 2).
Keep the action steps specific and in order.
Test with two different ingredient combinations.
successCriteria: Algorithm can be reused with different sandwich inputs.
Order of steps remains valid across both tests.
Team explains how input changes but algorithm structure stays stable.

lessonSlides.title: Get the Room Ready
whatToDo: Write these four words on the board — leave them visible all lesson:
Algorithm: a set of step-by-step instructions
Input: what goes in (ingredients, data, commands)
Output: what comes out (the sandwich, the result, the route)
Sequence: the order of steps

Place at the front table: a loaf of bread still in its bag (closed), a closed jar of peanut butter, a closed jar of jam, a knife, and a plate. Do not open anything yet — the sealed, untouched props are part of the activity.
Have paper and pencils ready for the pair-writing phase later.

lessonSlides.title: Ada Lovelace
lessonSlides.roleModel: Ada Lovelace (1815–1852) was a mathematician who worked with inventor Charles Babbage on his Analytical Engine — a mechanical computing machine. She wrote detailed instructions for how it could be used to calculate a complex mathematical sequence. Those instructions, written in 1843, are considered the world’s first computer program. Ada was the first person to understand that a machine could follow any logical sequence of steps — not just do maths. That idea is the foundation of every app, game, and website your students use today.
lessonSlides.whatToSay: Introduce Ada simply and warmly: “Today’s role model lived over 200 years ago — long before computers, smartphones, or the internet existed. Her name was Ada Lovelace. She wrote instructions for a machine that hadn’t even been built yet. We call those instructions an algorithm. And today, you are going to write one too.”
whatToDo: Keep the intro to about 60 seconds — enough to make it stick without slowing momentum.
Don’t feel you need to explain her full life story. One striking fact is enough: she wrote a computer program over 100 years before computers existed.
You can return to Ada naturally at these moments in the lesson:
Phase 3 (algorithm intro): ‘Ada Lovelace called these instructions an algorithm too — same word, same idea, just a different century.’
Phase 4 Round 2 (pairs writing): ‘Ada had exactly this problem — she had to write instructions so precise that a machine with no brain could follow them.’
Exit reflection: ‘Ada Lovelace did this with pen and paper in 1843. You just did it too.’
lessonSlides.realWorldConnection: Ada realised something no one else had seen: a machine that follows instructions precisely enough can do anything — not just calculate, but create music, process language, run a recommendation engine. Every algorithm powering technology today traces back to that idea. She named the concept. Your students are living it.

lessonSlides.phase: Role Model — Using Her in Class
lessonSlides.title: Bringing Ada Into the Lesson
lessonSlides.whatToSay: You do not need to give a long speech. A single well-placed sentence at the right moment lands far better than a long introduction. Here is a guide to the best natural moments to bring Ada in:
whatToDo: LESSON INTRO — right now: Keep it to one striking fact. ‘She wrote a computer program in 1843 — over 100 years before computers existed.’ That’s enough. Let the curiosity sit.

PHASE 3 — when you write the word ALGORITHM on the board: ‘Ada Lovelace used this exact word. She wrote an algorithm for a machine that hadn’t even been built yet. Same word. Same idea. Different century.’

PHASE 4 Round 2 — when pairs are struggling to be precise: ‘Ada had exactly this problem. She had to write instructions so specific that a machine with no brain could follow them. No guessing allowed. Sound familiar?’
PHASE 4 Round 3 — when you name debugging: ‘Finding and fixing errors in your instructions is called debugging. Ada did this too — she rewrote her algorithm multiple times before it was right.’

PHASE 5 — discussion, if time allows: ‘Ada wrote her algorithm for a machine that didn’t exist yet. Do you think it matters that she couldn’t test it? Can you solve a problem before you have the tools?’
EXIT REFLECTION — as the final word: ‘Ada Lovelace did exactly what you just did — with pen and paper, in 1843. Same idea. You just did it too.’
lessonSlides.realWorldConnection: Ada is not just a historical footnote. She understood something no one else did: that a machine could follow any logical sequence, not just do maths. That insight powers every algorithm running in the background of every app, recommendation engine, and search result your students use today. When they write an algorithm, they are continuing something she started.

lessonSlides.title: What We’re Doing Today
lessonSlides.whatToSay: Welcome the class and set the scene simply: “Today we are going to find out how computers actually think — or rather, how they don’t think at all. Computers don’t guess. They don’t use common sense. They can’t fill in the blanks. They follow instructions — exactly as written. And today, you are going to write some.”
whatToDo: Point to the vocabulary on the board: ‘These four words are what we’re working with today. You don’t need to know them yet — just notice they are there. By the end you will.’
Point to the PB&J materials at the front: ‘We are going to use those later. I’m not telling you what for yet.’
Let that sit for a moment. The mystery is part of the hook.
This is lesson one — students may not know what to expect. Keep the tone warm, simple, and a little playful. The lesson will explain itself through the activities.

lessonSlides.title: Spark Question
lessonSlides.whatToSay: Show this question on the board or read it aloud: “What do you think a robot could do better than a human, and what could it never do?”
whatToDo: Ask students to turn to the person next to them and share one idea each.
Take 3–4 answers from the class.
Write the 2 most interesting ones on the board — you will return to these at the end of the lesson.
Accept all answers without correcting. Nothing is wrong at this point.
lessonSlides.conversationStarter: If the class is quiet, try: ‘Think about something a machine does that would take a human hours — like calculating a million numbers. Now think about something even a toddler can do that a robot can’t — like knowing when someone is sad. What’s the difference between those two things?’ Wait for a few hands before taking answers.

lessonSlides.title: Robot Warm-Up
lessonSlides.whatToSay: “I am going to be a robot for the next 2 minutes. You need to give me instructions to walk from here to the door. But I only understand four commands: STEP FORWARD, TURN LEFT, TURN RIGHT, and STOP. Nothing else.”
whatToDo: Stand still and wait for the first instruction.
Follow every instruction exactly and literally — do not fill in any gaps.
If a student says ‘walk to the door’ — do nothing. That is not one of the four commands.
If they say ‘turn left’ when you are facing the wrong direction — turn left anyway.
If you bump into a desk because they forgot to tell you to turn — stop there and wait.
After 2 minutes, stop and ask: ‘What was hard about giving me instructions? What did you have to think about that you don’t normally think about when you walk somewhere?’

lessonSlides.title: What Is an Algorithm?
lessonSlides.whatToSay: Write ALGORITHM on the board. Say: “This sounds complicated but it describes something you already do every day. An algorithm is a set of step-by-step instructions that solves a problem or completes a task. The steps must be in the right order, and precise enough that anyone — or any machine — could follow them without guessing.”
whatToDo: Write these three examples on the board and explain each one out loud:
1. A recipe — if you add the eggs before mixing the flour, the cake comes out wrong. Order matters.
2. Your morning routine — you always put socks on before shoes. That is an algorithm.
3. LEGO instructions — if you skip step 14, the model falls apart in step 20. Each step depends on the one before it.
Point to the vocabulary on the board: ‘These are the words we’ll use today.’

Say: ‘Computers run on algorithms. Every single thing a computer does — playing a video, sending a message, showing a search result — is the result of someone writing very precise instructions. Today we are going to write one ourselves, and find out what happens when the instructions are not precise enough.’
lessonSlides.realWorldConnection: Ask: ‘Has a GPS ever given you directions that seemed wrong? Or has a streaming service recommended something you hate?’ Those are algorithm mistakes — the instructions were precise, but did not account for everything a human would notice. This is exactly the problem students are about to experience.
lessonSlides.conversationStarter: If students look blank, try: ‘You’ve already used an algorithm today. Did anyone eat breakfast? Did you put your shoes on before your socks? No — because there’s a sequence. That sequence is an algorithm. Same idea, just with food and shoes instead of code.’ Keep it concrete and personal.

lessonSlides.title: PB&J Robot Challenge — Setup
lessonSlides.whatToSay: “You are going to give me instructions to make a peanut butter and jam sandwich. I am the robot. I will follow your instructions exactly. I cannot guess what you mean. I cannot use common sense. If you do not tell me to do it, I will not do it.”
whatToDo: Place on the front table: bread still in its closed bag, closed jar of peanut butter, closed jar of jam, a knife, and a plate.
Make sure everything is still closed and in its original state.
Stand behind the table and wait for the class to begin giving instructions.

lessonSlides.title: The Class Gives Instructions
whatToDo: Ask for the first instruction from the class.
A student will probably say something like ‘put the peanut butter on the bread.’
Pick up the closed jar and place it on top of the closed bag of bread.
Say: ‘Is that right? I put the peanut butter on the bread. The jar is on the bag.’
When students protest, say: ‘I know what you meant. But I am a robot. I can only do exactly what you say.’
Do NOT open the jar until a student explicitly says ‘open the jar.’
Do NOT take bread out of the bag until told to do so.
If they say ‘spread the peanut butter’ without specifying which side — spread it on the outside crust.
Continue literally until the class fully understands the problem.
lessonSlides.conversationStarter: If students immediately give very precise instructions, commit anyway — find a small gap in their wording and act on it literally. For example: ‘Open the jar of peanut butter’ — open it, then stop and wait. They said open, not pick up a knife, not scoop anything. Follow the letter of what was said, not the intent.

lessonSlides.title: Pairs Write a Better Algorithm
lessonSlides.whatToSay: “Now you are going to fix this. In pairs, you have 8 minutes to write a perfect algorithm. It must be so precise that even a robot that cannot guess could follow it. Number every step. One action per step.”
whatToDo: Give each pair a sheet of paper.
Walk around the room and push students to be more precise.
Ask: ‘What if I don’t know what spread means?’
Ask: ‘What if I don’t know which side of the knife to use?’
Ask: ‘What if I don’t know where the bread is?’
Ask: ‘Did you remember to tell me to open the jar first?’
lessonSlides.conversationStarter: If a pair finishes quickly and thinks they’re done, push deeper: ‘What if the bread is still in the bag — did you say to take it out? What if the knife is in a drawer across the room — did you say to get it? What if I don’t know what a knife looks like?’ There is always a gap to find.

lessonSlides.title: Test One Algorithm — Debug Live
whatToDo: Ask for one volunteer pair to read their algorithm aloud, step by step.
Follow every step literally while the class watches.
The rest of the class can call out mistakes and suggest fixes in real time.
When the class spots a problem, ask: ‘How would you fix that step? What should the exact instruction be?’
You can name what’s happening: ‘What you are doing right now is called debugging. Professional programmers spend a large part of their day doing exactly this.’

lessonSlides.title: Discussion
whatToDo: Pick the question below that feels most alive based on how the class went.
One strong discussion is better than two rushed ones.
Give students a moment to think before taking answers.
Return to the 2 answers you wrote on the board at the start.
discussionQuestions: “A robot followed your instructions exactly and got the wrong result. Whose fault is that — the robot’s or the programmer’s?” Follow-up: “Does this change how you think about technology making mistakes? When a GPS takes you the wrong way, is that the GPS’s fault?”
“Go back to the question from the start — what can a robot do better than a human, and what can it never do? Has your answer changed?” Point to the answers you wrote at the beginning.
lessonSlides.conversationStarter: If the discussion goes quiet, try: ‘Think about the last time your phone did something annoying — like autocorrect changing a word to something completely wrong. Was that the phone’s fault? Or the fault of whoever wrote the instructions?’ Autocorrect usually gets a strong reaction from this age group.

lessonSlides.title: Write the Perfect Algorithm
lessonSlides.webappCue: Ask everyone to get their computers out and open the webapp. Wait until most students are on before you continue.
lessonSlides.whatToSay: “Working on your own, you are going to write a step-by-step algorithm for one of these tasks — your choice: getting ready for school in the morning, making a glass of chocolate milk, or feeding a pet. You’ll type it directly into the webapp.”
rules: Each step must be one action only — not two actions combined.
The algorithm must have at least 6 steps.
A robot that cannot guess must be able to follow it.
whatToDo: Give students a moment to get set up on the webapp — wait until most are ready before starting.
Walk around and watch for students who catch themselves writing vague steps and fix them.
When most students have a draft, ask them to swap with a partner on the webapp to review each other’s algorithms.
The partner marks any unclear step.
The original writer improves that step. This is the debugging phase.

lessonSlides.title: Sequence Sorting: Make Tea
lessonSlides.webappCue: Tell the class to stay on the webapp and open the next activity: Sequence Sorting.
lessonSlides.whatToSay: If students have finished Exercise 1 and time allows: “Stay on the webapp and open the next activity — Sequence Sorting. You will see six tea-making steps. Your job is to drag them into the correct order.”
whatToDo: Students open the Sequence Sorting activity on the webapp.
They drag the six tea-making steps into the order they think is correct.
Ask them to compare their sequence with a partner when done.
If they disagree on any step, ask them to explain why their sequence works.
lessonSlides.discussionClose: Ask: ‘Is there only one correct order? Or could some steps swap?’ Yes — ‘add milk’ could come before or after removing the teabag. This introduces the idea that some algorithms have flexibility built in, and some have none at all.

lessonSlides.title: Algorithm for a Robot Chef
lessonSlides.webappCue: Tell fast finishers to stay on the webapp and open the extension task.
lessonSlides.whatToSay: Tell fast finishers: “Stay on the webapp and open the extension task. A robot chef has been hired at the school canteen. It can only follow written instructions. It has no common sense — if something is not in the instructions, it will not do it. It also cannot taste food.”
lessonSlides.task: Write the algorithm for one item on today’s school lunch menu. It must be detailed enough that the robot could follow it without any help.
challengeQuestions: What would happen if a student had a nut allergy and the robot followed your algorithm — would they be safe? If not, how would you fix it?
What if the robot ran out of one ingredient halfway through — what should it do? How would you write that into the algorithm?
Can you write an algorithm that works for making ANY sandwich, not just one specific type? Try using placeholders like SPREAD A and SPREAD B.

lessonSlides.title: Close the Lesson
whatToDo: Ask 2–3 students to read one step from their algorithm aloud.
The class listens and raises a hand if they spot a step that is not precise enough.
Keep it short and warm — this is a light public share, not a formal presentation.
Ask every student — verbally or on a sticky note — to complete this sentence:
lessonSlides.exitReflection: "An algorithm needs to be _______ because computers cannot _______.
lessonSlides.whatToSay: After collecting responses or hearing 3–4 answers, close with: “Next week we will look at what computers are actually very good at — spotting patterns — and how that connects to something you use every day.”
